metadata.title: Coding Interview Patterns & Practice Problems
metadata.description: Comprehensive guide to all major coding interview patterns with C++ solutions. Covers arrays, trees, graphs, DP, and design problems for senior engineer positions at Nvidia, Google, Qualcomm, Ericsson, and Arista Networks.
metadata.targetAudience: Senior Software Engineers (5+ years) preparing for big tech coding interviews

chapters.title: Arrays & Hashing
chapters.description: Frequency counting, grouping, duplicate detection, and hash map patterns.
chapters.patternOverview: Arrays and hash maps are the foundation of most coding problems. Hash maps provide O(1) average lookup, enabling us to trade space for time. Key patterns: frequency counting, two-sum lookup, grouping by key.

patterns.name: Hash Map Lookup
patterns.description: Use a hash map to store seen elements for O(1) lookup, converting O(n²) brute force to O(n).
patterns.intuition: Instead of searching the entire array for a complement, store what you've seen in a map. When you encounter an element, check if its complement exists.

patterns.name: Frequency Counter
patterns.description: Count occurrences using hash map, then process counts.
patterns.intuition: Many problems reduce to: count everything first, then answer queries on the counts. Hash map makes counting O(n).
identificationSignals: Check if two strings are anagrams

patterns.name: Group By Key
patterns.description: Group elements sharing a common property using hash map of lists.
patterns.intuition: Transform each element into a canonical key, then group all elements with the same key together.
patterns.timeComplexity: O(n * k) where k = key computation cost

problems.title: Two Sum
problems.problemStatement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. Each input has exactly one solution.
constraints: Each input has exactly one solution
hints: Can you find the complement in O(1)?
Use a hash map to store values and indices

bruteForce.approach: Check all pairs with nested loops

optimalSolution.intuition: For each number, check if (target - number) was seen before.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Iterate through array. For each element, compute complement = target - nums[i]. If complement is in our map, we found the pair. Otherwise, store current element and index.

followUps: What if array is sorted? (Two pointers)
What if there are multiple solutions?

problems.title: Group Anagrams
problems.problemStatement: Given an array of strings, group the anagrams together. An anagram is a word formed by rearranging the letters of another.

examples.explanation: Words with same letters grouped together

constraints: strs[i] consists of lowercase English letters
hints: What's a good key for anagrams?
Sort each word, or use character frequency as key

bruteForce.approach: Compare all pairs for anagram check

optimalSolution.approach: Sort each word as key, group in hash map
optimalSolution.intuition: Anagrams have the same sorted form. Use sorted string as hash map key.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: For each string, sort it to create a canonical key. All anagrams will have the same sorted key. Group them in a hash map.

followUps: Can you use character count as key for O(n*k)?

problems.title: Top K Frequent Elements
problems.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements.
constraints: k is in range [1, number of unique elements]
hints: Count frequencies first
Use a heap or bucket sort

optimalSolution.intuition: Frequency can be at most n. Create buckets indexed by frequency, put elements in their frequency bucket, then collect from highest bucket.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Count frequencies with hash map. Create n+1 buckets. Place each number in bucket[its frequency]. Scan buckets from highest to lowest, collecting k elements.

followUps: Can you solve it with a min-heap in O(n log k)?

chapterQuiz.question: You need to find if any two elements in an array sum to a target. Which pattern should you use?
chapterQuiz.explanation: One-pass hash map gives O(n) time. Store each element and check for complement.

chapterQuiz.question: What is the average time complexity of hash map insertion and lookup?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Hash maps provide O(1) average for insert and lookup. Worst case is O(n) due to hash collisions.

chapterQuiz.question: To check if two strings are anagrams, the most efficient approach is:
options: Use frequency count array of size 26
chapterQuiz.correctAnswer: Use frequency count array of size 26
chapterQuiz.explanation: Frequency counting with a fixed-size array is O(n) time and O(1) space (26 chars). Sorting is O(n log n).

chapters.title: Two Pointers
chapters.description: Opposite direction, same direction, fast-slow pointer techniques for arrays and linked lists.
chapters.patternOverview: Two pointers use two indices/references to traverse data, reducing nested loops from O(n²) to O(n). Three variants: opposite direction (converging), same direction (fast-slow), and partitioning.

patterns.name: Opposite Direction
patterns.description: Two pointers start at opposite ends and move toward each other.
patterns.intuition: For sorted arrays: if sum too small, move left pointer right; if too large, move right pointer left. Each step eliminates possibilities, so O(n) total.

patterns.name: Fast-Slow (Floyd's)
patterns.description: Two pointers move at different speeds. Used for cycle detection and finding middle.
patterns.intuition: If there's a cycle, the fast pointer will eventually lap the slow pointer. For finding middle: when fast reaches end, slow is at middle.

patterns.name: Partitioning (Dutch National Flag)
patterns.description: Three-way partition using two pointers to categorize elements.
patterns.intuition: Maintain invariant: elements before low are category-1, between low and mid are category-2, after high are category-3.

problems.title: 3Sum
problems.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, return all triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j != k and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0.
hints: Sort first, then reduce to Two Sum II
Skip duplicates to avoid duplicate triplets

optimalSolution.intuition: Fix one element, use two pointers for the remaining pair (Two Sum II on sorted array). Skip duplicates.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Sort array. For each element i, use two pointers (left=i+1, right=end) to find pairs that sum to -nums[i]. Skip duplicate values.

followUps: 4Sum? Same approach with one more loop

problems.title: Container With Most Water
problems.problemStatement: Given n non-negative integers height[i], find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container that holds the most water.

examples.explanation: Lines at index 1 and 8 form container with area 7*7=49

hints: Start with widest container
Move the shorter line inward

optimalSolution.intuition: Start with widest container. Moving the shorter line inward might find a taller line. Moving the taller line can only decrease width without helping height.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Two pointers at ends. Calculate area. Move the pointer with shorter height inward (moving the taller one can't help). Track maximum.

chapterQuiz.question: Array is sorted. Find if any pair sums to target. Best approach?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Since array is already sorted, two pointers from ends gives O(n) time and O(1) space — better than hash map's O(n) space.

chapterQuiz.question: How do you detect a cycle in a linked list with O(1) space?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Fast-slow pointers detect cycles in O(n) time, O(1) space. Hash set works but uses O(n) space.

chapters.title: Sliding Window
chapters.description: Fixed-size and variable-size window techniques for substring and subarray problems.
chapters.patternOverview: Sliding window maintains a window [left, right] over a sequence, expanding and shrinking to find optimal subarrays/substrings. Avoids recomputation by adding/removing one element at a time.

patterns.name: Variable-Size Window
patterns.description: Expand right until constraint is met, then shrink left to optimize.
patterns.intuition: Grow the window by moving right. When a condition is violated (or met), shrink from left. Track the best window seen.
identificationSignals: Substring with at most k distinct characters

patterns.name: Fixed-Size Window
patterns.description: Slide a window of fixed size k across the array.
patterns.intuition: Maintain a window of exactly k elements. When moving right, add new element and remove leftmost. Compute statistic on each position.
identificationSignals: Maximum sum subarray of size k

problems.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
problems.problemStatement: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.
hints: Use a set to track characters in current window
Shrink window when duplicate found

optimalSolution.intuition: Expand right. If duplicate found, shrink from left until no duplicate. Window always contains unique characters.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Maintain a set of characters in the current window. Expand right by adding s[right]. If it's a duplicate, shrink from left until it's removed. Track max window size.

followUps: Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct Characters

problems.title: Minimum Window Substring
problems.problemStatement: Given strings s and t, return the minimum window substring of s such that every character in t (including duplicates) is included in the window.

examples.explanation: Minimum window containing A, B, and C

hints: Use frequency map for t
Expand right until all chars found, then shrink left

optimalSolution.intuition: Track how many characters from t are satisfied. Expand right until all satisfied, then shrink left to minimize window.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Count required characters from t. Expand right, tracking how many requirements are met. When all met, shrink from left to find minimum. Track best window.

followUps: What if we need to find all minimum windows?

chapterQuiz.question: Find longest subarray with sum <= k. Which pattern?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Variable window: expand right (add to sum), shrink left when sum > k. Track maximum window length.

chapterQuiz.question: Why is sliding window O(n) even though it has a nested while loop?
options: The while loop runs O(1) on average
Left pointer moves at most n times total
chapterQuiz.correctAnswer: Left pointer moves at most n times total
chapterQuiz.explanation: Each element is added and removed from the window at most once. Left pointer moves at most n times total across all iterations, so total work is O(n).

chapters.title: Binary Search
chapters.description: Classic binary search, search on answer, and rotated array patterns.
chapters.patternOverview: Binary search eliminates half the search space each step, giving O(log n) time. Beyond sorted arrays, it applies to any monotonic predicate — 'binary search on the answer'.
whenToUse: Minimize/maximize a value with monotonic feasibility

patterns.name: Standard Binary Search
patterns.description: Find target in sorted array or find insertion point.
patterns.intuition: Compare target with middle element. If equal, found. If less, search left half. If greater, search right half. Each comparison halves the search space.

patterns.name: Binary Search on Answer
patterns.description: Binary search on the result space when feasibility is monotonic.
patterns.intuition: If you can check whether an answer X is feasible in O(n), and feasibility is monotonic (all X >= answer are feasible, or all X <= answer are feasible), binary search on X.
identificationSignals: Can you do X in k steps?

problems.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
problems.problemStatement: Given a rotated sorted array (no duplicates) and a target, return its index or -1.
hints: One half is always sorted
Determine which half target could be in

optimalSolution.intuition: At each step, one half is sorted. Check if target is in the sorted half; if yes, search there, else search the other half.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: At each step: if left half is sorted (nums[lo] <= nums[mid]), check if target is in [lo, mid). If right half sorted, check [mid, hi]. Always search the correct half.

followUps: Find minimum in rotated sorted array (LC #153)

problems.title: Koko Eating Bananas
problems.problemStatement: Koko can eat at speed k bananas/hour. Given piles and h hours, find minimum k to eat all bananas in h hours.
hints: Binary search on the eating speed k
Check if speed k allows finishing in h hours

bruteForce.approach: Try every speed from 1 to max(piles)

optimalSolution.intuition: If speed k works, any speed > k also works (monotonic). Binary search for minimum k where total hours <= h.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Binary search on speed [1, max(piles)]. For each speed mid, calculate total hours. If <= h, try smaller speed (hi=mid). Else need faster (lo=mid+1).

chapterQuiz.question: Minimize the maximum load across k workers. Which pattern?
chapterQuiz.explanation: If max load X is feasible with k workers, any X' > X is also feasible. Binary search for minimum feasible X.

chapterQuiz.question: Why use mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2 instead of (lo + hi) / 2?
chapterQuiz.explanation: If lo and hi are large, (lo + hi) can overflow int. lo + (hi - lo) / 2 is equivalent but safe.

chapters.title: Stack
chapters.description: Monotonic stack, expression evaluation, and histogram-based problems.
chapters.patternOverview: Stacks maintain LIFO ordering. Monotonic stacks keep elements in sorted order, efficiently finding the next greater/smaller element. Key for histogram, temperature, and expression problems.

patterns.name: Monotonic Stack
patterns.description: Stack maintains elements in increasing or decreasing order.
patterns.intuition: When we encounter an element, pop all elements from stack that violate the monotonic property. Each popped element has found its 'next greater/smaller'.

problems.title: Valid Parentheses
problems.problemStatement: Given a string containing just '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.
hints: Push opening brackets
Pop and match for closing brackets

optimalSolution.intuition: Push open brackets. For close brackets, check if top of stack matches. Valid if stack is empty at end.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: For each character: if opening bracket, push. If closing bracket, check stack top matches. Return true if stack empty at end.

followUps: Minimum Remove to Make Valid Parentheses

problems.title: Daily Temperatures
problems.problemStatement: Given daily temperatures, for each day find how many days until a warmer temperature. Return 0 if no warmer day.
hints: Use a decreasing monotonic stack
Stack stores indices of unresolved days

optimalSolution.intuition: Stack holds indices of days waiting for a warmer day. When we find a warmer day, pop all cooler days from stack.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Maintain stack of indices with decreasing temperatures. For each new temperature, pop all colder days from stack — those days have found their answer.

chapterQuiz.question: For each element, find the next element that is greater. Best approach?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Monotonic decreasing stack: pop smaller elements when larger element found. Each element is pushed/popped once = O(n).

chapterQuiz.question: Why is the monotonic stack pattern O(n) despite the nested while loop?
options: Each element is pushed and popped at most once
The while loop runs O(1) times
chapterQuiz.explanation: Each element is pushed once and popped once, so total operations across all iterations = O(n). This is amortized O(1) per element.

chapters.title: Linked Lists
chapters.description: Reversal, cycle detection, merging, and advanced linked list techniques.
chapters.patternOverview: Linked list problems test pointer manipulation skills. Key patterns: in-place reversal, fast-slow pointer (Floyd's), merge two sorted, and dummy head technique.
whenToUse: Reverse a portion of a list
Find middle or kth from end

patterns.name: In-Place Reversal
patterns.description: Reverse a linked list by rewiring pointers.
patterns.intuition: Maintain three pointers: prev, curr, next. For each node: save next, point curr to prev, advance prev and curr.

problems.title: Reverse Linked List
problems.problemStatement: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list, and return the reversed list.
hints: Use three pointers
Or solve recursively

optimalSolution.intuition: Walk through list, reversing each pointer. prev starts at null, becomes the new head.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Three pointers: prev=null, curr=head. Each step: save next, reverse pointer, advance. When done, prev is new head.

followUps: Reverse between positions m and n

problems.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
problems.problemStatement: Merge two sorted linked lists and return as one sorted list.
hints: Use a dummy head node
Compare and pick smaller

optimalSolution.intuition: Use dummy node to simplify head handling. Compare nodes from both lists, append smaller one.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Dummy node avoids special-casing the head. Compare front nodes of both lists, attach smaller one to result tail. Append remaining nodes.

chapterQuiz.question: Check if a linked list is a palindrome in O(1) space?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Find middle with slow-fast. Reverse second half. Compare both halves node by node. O(n) time, O(1) space.

chapterQuiz.question: Why use a dummy head node when building a linked list?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Dummy node means you never have to special-case 'head is null'. Just return dummy.next at the end.

chapters.title: Trees
chapters.description: DFS/BFS traversals, LCA, diameter, serialize/deserialize, and path problems.
chapters.patternOverview: Tree problems are recursive by nature. DFS explores depth-first (preorder, inorder, postorder). BFS goes level by level. Most tree problems reduce to: what information do I need from my left and right children?

patterns.name: DFS (Recursive/Iterative)
patterns.description: Explore tree depth-first. Three orderings: preorder (root-left-right), inorder (left-root-right), postorder (left-right-root).
patterns.intuition: At each node, decide what to do before/after visiting children. Preorder = process then recurse. Postorder = recurse then combine.

patterns.name: BFS (Level Order)
patterns.description: Process tree level by level using a queue.
patterns.intuition: Queue holds all nodes at current level. Process them, add their children (next level). Track level boundaries.

problems.title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
problems.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth.
hints: Depth = 1 + max(left depth, right depth)

bruteForce.approach: Same as optimal for this problem

optimalSolution.intuition: A tree's depth = 1 + max(left subtree depth, right subtree depth). Base case: null node has depth 0.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Base case: null returns 0. Each node returns 1 + max of children's depths. This naturally computes the maximum depth.

problems.title: Lowest Common Ancestor
problems.problemStatement: Given a binary tree, find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two given nodes.
constraints: p and q exist in the tree
hints: If current node is p or q, it could be the LCA
If p and q are on different sides, current node is LCA

bruteForce.approach: Find paths to both nodes, compare

optimalSolution.intuition: If a node finds p in left subtree and q in right (or vice versa), it's the LCA. If it IS p or q, return itself.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: If root is null or p or q, return root. Recurse left and right. If both return non-null, root is LCA. Otherwise return the non-null side.

chapterQuiz.question: Print tree nodes level by level. Which traversal?
chapterQuiz.explanation: BFS with a queue naturally processes nodes level by level.

chapterQuiz.question: Inorder traversal of a BST gives elements in what order?
chapterQuiz.explanation: BST property: left < root < right. Inorder visits left, root, right — producing sorted order.

chapters.title: Dynamic Programming — 1D
chapters.description: Climbing stairs, house robber, coin change, LIS, and word break patterns.
chapters.patternOverview: DP solves problems with optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems. Define state, recurrence relation, and base cases. Two approaches: top-down (memoization) and bottom-up (tabulation).

patterns.name: Take or Skip
patterns.description: At each position, decide to take (and skip adjacent) or skip current.
patterns.intuition: dp[i] = max(take current + dp[i-2], skip current + dp[i-1]). Classic for non-adjacent selection problems.

patterns.name: Coin Change / Unbounded Knapsack
patterns.description: Find minimum coins / count ways to make an amount.
patterns.intuition: dp[amount] = min over all coins c: dp[amount - c] + 1. Build solutions for small amounts first.

problems.title: House Robber
problems.problemStatement: Given an array representing money in houses along a street, find maximum money you can rob without robbing adjacent houses.
hints: For each house: rob it + best from 2 back, or skip it

bruteForce.approach: Try all subsets of non-adjacent houses

optimalSolution.intuition: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]). Only need last two values.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: At each house: either skip it (take prev1) or rob it (prev2 + current). Only need two previous values, so O(1) space.

problems.title: Coin Change
problems.problemStatement: Given coins of different denominations and an amount, return fewest coins needed. Return -1 if not possible.
hints: Build solutions bottom-up from 0 to amount
dp[i] = min(dp[i-c] + 1) for each coin c

optimalSolution.intuition: dp[i] = minimum coins to make amount i. For each amount, try each coin and take the minimum.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: dp[0]=0. For each amount i from 1 to target: try each coin c<=i, take min(dp[i], dp[i-c]+1). If dp[amount] unchanged, return -1.

chapterQuiz.question: Cannot select adjacent elements, maximize sum. Which DP pattern?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Classic take-or-skip: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]).

chapterQuiz.question: Top-down vs Bottom-up DP: which is generally more space efficient?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Bottom-up allows space optimization (rolling arrays). Top-down uses recursion stack + memo table.

chapters.title: Dynamic Programming — 2D
chapters.description: Grid paths, edit distance, LCS, knapsack, and matrix-based DP.
chapters.patternOverview: 2D DP uses a table where dp[i][j] represents the solution for a subproblem defined by two parameters (e.g., two string indices, grid position, items and capacity).

patterns.name: Two Strings DP
patterns.description: Compare two strings character by character, building solutions in a 2D table.
patterns.intuition: dp[i][j] = answer for first i chars of s1 and first j chars of s2. Transition depends on whether s1[i-1] == s2[j-1].

patterns.name: 0/1 Knapsack
patterns.description: Select items (each used at most once) to maximize value within capacity.
patterns.intuition: dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with capacity w. Either include item i (if it fits) or exclude it.

problems.title: Longest Common Subsequence
problems.problemStatement: Given two strings, return the length of their longest common subsequence.
hints: If chars match, extend LCS
If not, take best of excluding either char

optimalSolution.intuition: If chars match, LCS extends by 1. If not, take max of excluding last char from either string.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Build table bottom-up. dp[i][j] = LCS of t1[0..i-1] and t2[0..j-1]. If chars match: diagonal + 1. Else: max of left or above.

problems.title: Edit Distance
problems.problemStatement: Given two strings, return minimum operations (insert, delete, replace) to convert word1 to word2.
hints: If chars match, no operation needed
Otherwise try insert, delete, replace

optimalSolution.intuition: dp[i][j] = min ops for word1[0..i-1] to word2[0..j-1]. If chars match: dp[i-1][j-1]. Else: 1 + min(insert, delete, replace).
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Base: converting empty string to length-j string needs j insertions. dp[i-1][j]=delete, dp[i][j-1]=insert, dp[i-1][j-1]=replace.

chapterQuiz.question: Find minimum operations to transform one string to another. Pattern?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Edit Distance is the classic Two Strings DP problem. dp[i][j] = min operations for first i chars of s1 to first j chars of s2.

chapterQuiz.question: Can 2D DP tables always be optimized to 1D?
options: Only if each row depends only on the previous row
chapterQuiz.correctAnswer: Only if each row depends only on the previous row
chapterQuiz.explanation: If dp[i][j] only depends on dp[i-1][...] (previous row), we can use two 1D arrays or even one with careful ordering.

chapters.title: Graphs — BFS & DFS
chapters.description: Connected components, shortest path, topological sort, and cycle detection.
chapters.patternOverview: Graphs model relationships. BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs. DFS explores all reachable nodes and detects cycles. Build adjacency list from edges, then traverse.

patterns.name: BFS Shortest Path
patterns.description: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs.
patterns.intuition: BFS explores all nodes at distance d before distance d+1. First time we reach a node, it's via the shortest path.

patterns.name: Topological Sort
patterns.description: Order nodes in a DAG so all edges point forward.
patterns.intuition: BFS (Kahn's): start with in-degree 0 nodes. Remove them, reduce neighbors' in-degree. Repeat. If all nodes processed, no cycle.

problems.title: Number of Islands
problems.problemStatement: Given a 2D grid of '1's (land) and '0's (water), count the number of islands.
hints: Each island is a connected component
DFS/BFS from each unvisited land cell

optimalSolution.intuition: Iterate grid. When you find a '1', that's a new island. DFS to mark all connected '1's as visited. Count DFS starts.
optimalSolution.spaceComplexity: O(m * n) recursion stack worst case
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Scan grid. Each '1' starts a new island. DFS marks all connected land as visited ('0'). Count how many DFS calls we make.

problems.title: Course Schedule
problems.problemStatement: There are numCourses courses with prerequisites. Determine if you can finish all courses.
hints: Model as directed graph
Check for cycles using topological sort

optimalSolution.intuition: Build prerequisite graph. Start with courses having no prerequisites (in-degree 0). Process them, reduce in-degrees. If all courses processed, possible.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Build adjacency list and in-degree array. Start with zero in-degree nodes. Process each, decrement neighbors' in-degree. If count == n, no cycle.

chapterQuiz.question: Check if a course schedule is possible (no circular dependencies). Pattern?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Course schedule is a DAG problem. Topological sort detects cycles — if not all nodes are processed, a cycle exists.

chapterQuiz.question: BFS/DFS on a graph with V vertices and E edges has time complexity:
chapterQuiz.explanation: We visit each vertex once O(V) and explore each edge once O(E), giving O(V + E) total.

chapters.title: Backtracking
chapters.description: Subsets, permutations, combinations, N-Queens, and constraint satisfaction problems.
chapters.patternOverview: Backtracking explores all possible solutions by building candidates incrementally and abandoning (backtracking) when constraints are violated. Think of it as DFS on a decision tree.

patterns.name: Subsets/Combinations
patterns.description: For each element: include or exclude it. Generates 2^n subsets.
patterns.intuition: At each position, branch into two paths: take the element or skip it. Base case: reached end of array, add current subset to results.

problems.title: Subsets
problems.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets.
hints: For each element: include or exclude
Or iterate and add to existing subsets

bruteForce.approach: Same as optimal for this problem

optimalSolution.intuition: At each index, decide to include or exclude the current element. This generates all 2^n combinations.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Start with empty set. At each level, try adding each remaining element. After recursion, remove last element (backtrack). Every intermediate state is a valid subset.

problems.title: Permutations
problems.problemStatement: Given an array of distinct integers, return all possible permutations.
hints: Use a boolean visited array
Or swap elements

optimalSolution.intuition: At each position, try every unused element. Mark as used, recurse, then unmark (backtrack).
optimalSolution.walkthrough: At each level, try all unused elements. When permutation is complete (size == n), add to results. Backtrack by removing and unmarking.

chapterQuiz.question: How many subsets does an array of n elements have?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Each element is either included or excluded: 2 choices per element = 2^n total subsets.

chapterQuiz.question: Generate all valid arrangements of n queens on n×n board. Pattern?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Place queens row by row. At each row, try each column. If valid (no conflicts), recurse. Otherwise backtrack.

chapters.title: Greedy Algorithms
chapters.description: Interval scheduling, jump game, and local-optimal-leads-to-global-optimal patterns.
chapters.patternOverview: Greedy makes the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping to find the global optimum. Works when the problem has the greedy choice property and optimal substructure.
whenToUse: Minimum operations with clear greedy choice

patterns.name: Interval Scheduling
patterns.description: Sort intervals and process greedily.
patterns.intuition: For merge: sort by start, merge overlapping. For max non-overlapping: sort by end, pick earliest finishing.

problems.title: Merge Intervals
problems.problemStatement: Given an array of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals.
hints: Sort by start time
Check if current overlaps with last merged

optimalSolution.intuition: Sort by start. If current interval overlaps with last merged (start <= last.end), extend last. Otherwise, add new.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Sort intervals by start time. For each interval: if it doesn't overlap with the last merged interval, add it. Otherwise, extend the last merged interval's end.

chapterQuiz.question: Maximize number of non-overlapping intervals. Sort by?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Sort by end time, pick earliest finishing interval. This leaves maximum room for remaining intervals (Activity Selection).

chapterQuiz.question: How do you know when greedy works vs when DP is needed?
options: Greedy works when locally optimal choice is globally optimal
chapterQuiz.correctAnswer: Greedy works when locally optimal choice is globally optimal
chapterQuiz.explanation: Greedy requires the greedy choice property. If choices interact in complex ways (e.g., 0/1 knapsack), DP is needed.

chapters.title: Bit Manipulation Problems
chapters.description: XOR tricks, counting bits, and bitmask-based problem solving.
chapters.patternOverview: Bit manipulation problems exploit properties of XOR (a^a=0, a^0=a), bit counting, and bitmask representation of sets.

patterns.name: XOR Tricks
patterns.description: XOR cancels duplicates (a^a=0) and is its own inverse.
patterns.intuition: XOR all elements: duplicates cancel out, leaving the unique element. Works for finding single numbers in duplicate arrays.
identificationSignals: Find element appearing once when others appear twice

problems.title: Single Number
problems.problemStatement: Every element appears twice except one. Find the single element.
hints: XOR of a number with itself is 0

optimalSolution.intuition: a^a=0, a^0=a. XOR all elements: pairs cancel, unique element remains.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: XOR all elements together. Since x^x=0 and x^0=x, all paired elements cancel out, leaving the single element.

chapterQuiz.question: What is the result of: 5 ^ 3 ^ 5?
chapterQuiz.explanation: 5^5=0, 0^3=3. XOR is commutative and associative, and a^a=0.

chapters.title: String Algorithms
chapters.description: Pattern matching (KMP), palindromes, and anagram-based problems.
chapters.patternOverview: String problems involve pattern matching, palindrome detection, and character manipulation. KMP avoids redundant comparisons. Expand-around-center finds palindromes efficiently.

patterns.name: Expand Around Center
patterns.description: For palindromes: expand from each center position outward.
patterns.intuition: Each palindrome has a center (single char for odd length, between two chars for even). Try each center, expand while characters match.

problems.title: Longest Palindromic Substring
problems.problemStatement: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring.
hints: Expand around each center
Consider both odd and even length palindromes

optimalSolution.intuition: Try each index as center of odd-length palindrome and each pair as center of even-length. Expand outward while chars match.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: For each index, expand outward for both odd and even length palindromes. Track longest found.

chapterQuiz.question: What is the time complexity of KMP pattern matching?
chapterQuiz.explanation: KMP preprocesses the pattern in O(m) and searches in O(n), where n = text length and m = pattern length.

chapters.title: Heap / Priority Queue
chapters.description: Top-K elements, merge K sorted, median finder, and scheduling problems.
chapters.patternOverview: Heaps efficiently track min/max elements. Min-heap for 'top K largest' (keep K elements, reject smaller). Max-heap for 'top K smallest'. Two heaps for running median.

patterns.name: Top-K Pattern
patterns.description: Use min-heap of size K to find K largest elements.
patterns.intuition: Maintain a min-heap of size K. For each new element: if larger than heap top, replace. Heap always contains K largest seen so far.

problems.title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
problems.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element.
hints: Min-heap of size k
Or use quickselect for average O(n)

optimalSolution.intuition: Maintain min-heap of size k. After processing all elements, top of heap is kth largest.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Push each element into min-heap. If heap size exceeds k, pop smallest. After all elements, heap contains k largest. Top = kth largest.

chapterQuiz.question: To find the Kth largest, should you use a min-heap or max-heap of size K?
chapterQuiz.explanation: Min-heap of size K: top element is the smallest of the K largest = Kth largest. When a larger element comes, the Kth largest naturally floats down.

chapters.title: Union Find / Disjoint Set
chapters.description: Connected components, redundant connection, and dynamic connectivity problems.
chapters.patternOverview: Union-Find efficiently tracks connected components with near O(1) union and find operations using path compression and union by rank.

patterns.name: Union-Find with Path Compression
patterns.description: Track disjoint sets with efficient union and find.
patterns.intuition: Each element points to a parent. Find follows parents to root (with path compression). Union merges two roots (by rank for balance).

problems.title: Number of Provinces
problems.problemStatement: Given n cities and a connectivity matrix, find the number of provinces (connected components).
hints: Union cities that are connected
Count distinct roots

optimalSolution.intuition: Union all connected pairs. Count distinct roots = number of provinces.
optimalSolution.walkthrough: Initialize each city as its own component. Union connected pairs. Count nodes that are their own root = number of components.

chapterQuiz.question: What is the amortized time complexity of Union-Find with path compression and union by rank?
chapterQuiz.explanation: α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function, which is effectively constant (< 5) for all practical input sizes.

chapters.title: Design Problems in Coding Rounds
chapters.description: LRU Cache, HashMap design, and iterator patterns for senior-level coding rounds.
chapters.patternOverview: Senior interviews include design problems in coding rounds: implement data structures with specific time complexity guarantees. These test both coding skill and system design intuition.

patterns.name: Hash Map + Doubly Linked List
patterns.description: Combine O(1) lookup with O(1) ordered operations.
patterns.intuition: Hash map gives O(1) access by key. Doubly linked list maintains order (LRU = least recently used at tail). Map values point to list nodes.

problems.title: LRU Cache
problems.problemStatement: Design a data structure for Least Recently Used (LRU) cache with O(1) get and put.

examples.explanation: After putting 3, key 2 was evicted (least recently used)

hints: HashMap for O(1) lookup
Doubly linked list for O(1) reorder

optimalSolution.intuition: Map stores key -> list node. List maintains usage order. On access: move to front. On eviction: remove from back.
optimalSolution.timeComplexity: O(1) for both get and put
optimalSolution.walkthrough: get: lookup in map, move node to front of list (splice). put: if exists, update and move to front. If new and full, evict back of list. Insert at front.

chapterQuiz.question: Why does LRU Cache use a doubly linked list instead of singly linked?
options: Need O(1) removal of any node
chapterQuiz.correctAnswer: Need O(1) removal of any node
chapterQuiz.explanation: Removing a node from a singly linked list requires O(n) to find the previous node. Doubly linked list has prev pointer for O(1) removal.
